vulnerability.title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
vulnerability.description: Configure the operating system to elevate the SELinux context when an administrator calls the sudo command. Edit a file in the /etc/sudoers.d directory with the following command: sudo visudo -f /etc/sudoers.d/CUSTOM_FILE Use the following example to build the CUSTOM_FILE in the /etc/sudoers.d directory to allow any administrator belonging to a designated sudoers admin group to elevate their SELinux context with the use of the sudo command: %wheel ALL=(ALL) TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r ALL
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command

triage_decision.reason: The remediation involves creating or editing a file in /etc/sudoers.d, a standard procedure for sudo configuration. Using visudo provides syntax checking, minimizing the risk of lockout. The change follows typical STIG hardening patterns and does not modify critical system services, bootloader, or filesystems.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential sudo syntax error leading to sudo command failure; Incorrect SELinux role/type may affect privilege escalation behavior

previous_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability openscap_017 not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

llm_verdict.message: Happy to hear the fix worked! If you have any other OpenSCAP findings, security hardening tasks, or configuration tweaks you’d like to address, just let me know—I’m ready to help.

review_feedback: The proposed fix adds the required SELinux TYPE attribute but also introduces a dangerous Defaults line that disables password authentication and logging for the admin group. This weakens sudo security and is not required by the OpenSCAP rule. Additionally, the example uses an unspecified admin_t type instead of the policy‑specific sysadm_t/sysadm_r pair, which may not satisfy the rule and could cause SELinux denials. Because the remediation degrades overall security, it should not be approved in its current form. Suggestions: Remove the overly permissive Defaults line; keep only the necessary SELinux attribute.; Use the exact TYPE and ROLE values required by the policy, e.g., TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r.; Validate the correct admin group name (e.g., %wheel) and limit the rule to that group.; Ensure the sudoers fragment has correct permissions (0440) and is owned by root.; Test the change on a non‑production system and verify SELinux context changes with `id -Z` after sudo.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The proposed fix adds the required SELinux TYPE attribute but also introduces a dangerous Defaults line that disables password authentication and logging for the admin group. This weakens sudo security and is not required by the OpenSCAP rule. Additionally, the example uses an unspecified admin_t type instead of the policy‑specific sysadm_t/sysadm_r pair, which may not satisfy the rule and could cause SELinux denials. Because the remediation degrades overall security, it should not be approved in its current form.
concerns: Defaults line disables password prompts (!authenticate) and logging (!log_input, !log_output) for the admin group, reducing accountability.
Use of an unspecified TYPE=admin_t may not match the required sysadm_t/sysadm_r context, leading to non‑compliance or SELinux denials.
Unnecessary inclusion of many Defaults options adds complexity and potential misconfiguration.
Potential for lock‑out if the sudoers fragment contains syntax errors, though visudo mitigates this.
suggested_improvements: Remove the overly permissive Defaults line; keep only the necessary SELinux attribute.
Use the exact TYPE and ROLE values required by the policy, e.g., TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r.
Validate the correct admin group name (e.g., %wheel) and limit the rule to that group.
Ensure the sudoers fragment has correct permissions (0440) and is owned by root.
Test the change on a non‑production system and verify SELinux context changes with `id -Z` after sudo.
